title: 26_400_bad_request.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Registration Always Fails With 400 — But the Form Looks Fine

A registration form submits an email and password to `/api/register`. The form passes client-side validation (email format looks right, password is over 8 characters), but the server always responds with `400 Bad Request` and the message `"confirm_password is required"`.

The developer is confused: "I didn't add a confirm password field — why is the server asking for one?"

A **400 Bad Request** means the client sent something the server couldn't use. The problem is always on the **request side** — either a missing field, a wrong field name, or a value that fails server-side rules. Fix the payload to match what the server expects.
traceback:
Browser Network tab:
  POST /api/register  400 Bad Request  22ms
  Request body: {"email":"alice@example.com","password":"hunter2!"}
  Response body: {"message":"confirm_password is required"}

UI shows: confirm_password is required

The server requires three fields: email, password, confirm_password.
The frontend sends only two. 400 = client sent wrong/incomplete data.
correctOutput:
Browser Network tab:
  POST /api/register  201 Created  34ms
  Response body: {"id": 8821, "email": "alice@example.com"}

UI shows: Registered! (alert dialog)

options.label: Add a `confirmPassword` field to the form and include it in the payload
options.resultLog:
Browser Network tab:
  POST /api/register  201 Created  34ms
  Request body: {"email":"alice@example.com","password":"hunter2!","confirmPassword":"hunter2!"}
  Response: {"id":8821,"email":"alice@example.com"}

UI: Registered!

The payload now matches the server's expected contract.
Client-side password match check added as UX guard.
options.rationale: A 400 means the request body doesn't match the server's contract. The server error message `'confirm_password is required'` is the exact field name it expects. The fix is to match the contract: add the third field the server requires, add a UI input for it, and include it in the JSON body. Reading the server's error message is the key skill — it told you exactly what was missing.

options.label: Catch the 400 and retry the request once automatically
options.resultLog:
Browser Network tab:
  POST /api/register  400 Bad Request  22ms
  POST /api/register  400 Bad Request  21ms  ← retry

UI shows: confirm_password is required

Two identical 400 responses. Retrying doesn't change the payload —
the server will return 400 forever until the payload is corrected.
options.rationale: Retrying is appropriate for **transient** errors: network timeouts, 503 Service Unavailable, 429 Too Many Requests. A **400 Bad Request** is deterministic — the payload is wrong and retrying with the same payload produces the same 400. You're just hitting the server twice for no benefit. The fix for a 400 is always to correct the request, not to repeat it.

options.label: Ignore the 400 and redirect to the dashboard anyway
options.resultLog:
Browser Network tab:
  POST /api/register  400 Bad Request  22ms

Browser redirects to /dashboard.
/dashboard tries to load user data — user doesn't exist in the database.
User sees an error or empty dashboard. Account was never created.

The 400 happened; the account wasn't saved; the redirect pretends it worked.
options.rationale: Ignoring the response status and redirecting creates a broken user experience: the registration silently failed but the user is redirected to an authenticated area. The database never created the account, so any attempt to load user data will fail or return empty results — and the user doesn't know why. **Never ignore response codes.** A 400 is the server telling you the operation failed. Handle it: show the error message, fix the payload.

hints: Open the Network tab and look at the response body for the 400 request. The server usually tells you exactly what was wrong — read the error message in the response.

The error message says `confirm_password is required`. The server expects three fields in the payload. Add a `confirmPassword` field to the state and include `confirmPassword` in `JSON.stringify(...)` on line 14.
explanationMd:
### HTTP status code families — who's at fault?
HTTP status codes are grouped into families by the first digit:
- **1xx** (Informational) — the request was received, continuing
- **2xx** (Success) — the request was accepted and processed
- **3xx** (Redirection) — the resource has moved; follow the redirect
- **4xx** (Client error) — **the client sent something wrong**; server refused it
- **5xx** (Server error) — the server failed while processing a valid request

This mental model matters for debugging: **4xx = your request is wrong**, **5xx = the server is broken**.

### 400 Bad Request — what it means
`400` means the server received the request but couldn't process it because the request data is malformed, missing, or invalid. Common causes:
- Missing required field
- Wrong field name (snake_case vs camelCase — `confirm_password` vs `confirmPassword`)
- Invalid value format (`email` that isn't an email address)
- Wrong field type (sending a string where a number was expected)

**The server's response body almost always tells you exactly what's wrong.** Always read `response.json()` on a 4xx response to get the error message.

### Reading server error messages
```js
if (!res.ok) {
  const body = await res.json();
  throw new Error(body.message ?? body.error ?? `HTTP ${res.status}`);
}
```
This pattern reads the server's error message from the response body. The server often provides the exact field name or rule that failed — `"confirm_password is required"` in this case. That's your fix.

### 4xx vs 5xx — when to retry
| Code | Meaning | Retry? |
|---|---|---|
| 400 | Request is wrong | No — fix the request |
| 401 | Not authenticated | No — log in first |
| 403 | Not authorised | No — different user/permissions |
| 404 | Not found | No — URL or resource is wrong |
| 429 | Rate limited | Yes — with backoff |
| 500 | Server crashed | Sometimes — if transient |
| 503 | Server unavailable | Yes — wait and retry |
recruiterReview: Correct. The key skill here is reading the server's error message — 'confirm_password is required' is a complete diagnosis. The retry distractor catches people who think 'maybe it's a fluke' — but 400 is deterministic, retrying is wasted effort. The ignore-and-redirect distractor is the worst possible outcome: the user thinks they registered, the account doesn't exist. Reading status codes and response bodies is table-stakes for any frontend engineer. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## HTTP Status Codes — Who's At Fault?

### The status code families
Every HTTP response has a 3-digit status code. The first digit tells you the category:

| Family | Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Informational | 100–199 | Rarely seen; protocol-level signalling |
| Success | 200–299 | The request worked |
| Redirection | 300–399 | The resource has moved; follow the redirect |
| Client error | 400–499 | **Your request was wrong** |
| Server error | 500–599 | **The server failed** |

The most important mental model: **4xx = your fault**, **5xx = server's fault**.

### The most common status codes
**2xx — Success:**
- `200 OK` — standard success (GET, PUT, PATCH)
- `201 Created` — resource was created (POST)
- `204 No Content` — success, no body returned (DELETE)

**4xx — Client errors (fix your request):**
- `400 Bad Request` — malformed body, missing field, invalid value
- `401 Unauthorized` — you must log in first
- `403 Forbidden` — logged in but not allowed to do this
- `404 Not Found` — wrong URL or resource doesn't exist
- `415 Unsupported Media Type` — wrong Content-Type header
- `422 Unprocessable Entity` — data parsed but failed validation
- `429 Too Many Requests` — you're sending too many requests

**5xx — Server errors (the server is broken):**
- `500 Internal Server Error` — the server threw an unhandled exception
- `502 Bad Gateway` — the server got a bad response from an upstream service
- `503 Service Unavailable` — the server is overloaded or down for maintenance

### How to read the error body
For 4xx responses, the server usually tells you what's wrong:
```js
const res = await fetch("/api/register", {
  method: "POST",
  headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
  body: JSON.stringify(payload),
});
if (!res.ok) {
  const error = await res.json();
  console.log(error);  // {"message": "confirm_password is required"}
}
```
Always check `response.json()` (or `response.text()`) when a request fails — the server's message is your diagnostic.

### When to retry
- **Never retry 4xx errors** (except 429) — the same bad request will always produce the same bad result. Fix the request.
- **Retry 429** — you hit a rate limit. Wait (check the `Retry-After` header if present) and try again.
- **Retry 503** — server is temporarily overloaded. Wait and retry with exponential backoff.
- **Retry 500 cautiously** — only if the server indicates it's transient; otherwise investigate server logs.

### You just learned
4xx status codes mean the **client** sent something wrong. 400 Bad Request means the payload doesn't match the server's contract. Always read the response body for 4xx errors — the server usually tells you exactly what's wrong.
